sources.notes: Read the same game rules and constraints as E1, with the hard-version requirement to output every winning first node.
Official editorial checked; the embedded hard-version code confirms a descending-weight sweep over Euler positions with an ordered set and Fenwick-maintained constraints.
Used to cross-check the necessary-and-sufficient condition via g(v), the LCA of strictly higher nodes outside subtree(v), and ancestor-union constraints.
Used to verify the O(n log n) implementation: two Fenwick trees, Euler-order extrema from a set, and +1 at v and g(v) minus 1 at LCA(v,g(v)).
Re-derived the characterization, matched it against recursive optimal play on 90,000 random small trees, and separately validated the LCA/Fenwick constraint sweep on 95,000 random trees.

reference_candidates.notes: Candidate Luogu solution index; not needed after the official code and two detailed independent writeups were checked.

annotation.summary: Output all first nodes u for which Cirno wins the increasing-weight subtree-deletion game under optimal play, rather than only one witness as in E1.
annotation.constraints: n <= 4e5 per test case, total n <= 4e5, and 1 <= w_i <= n. Enumerating a fresh residual game for every u is impossible; all candidates must be certified together in O(n log n).
annotation.core_idea: A candidate u must first have a strictly higher node outside subtree(u), otherwise the opponent has no move and wins. For every legal opponent reply v outside subtree(u) with w_v > w_u, Cirno wins exactly when every node x with w_x > w_v lies in subtree(u) or subtree(v). If this fails, choosing the maximum-weight violating v is a winning reply for the opponent by the same extremal argument as E1. Let H_v be the strictly higher nodes outside subtree(v), and when H_v is nonempty let g(v)=LCA(H_v). Then each higher v imposes the condition that u is an ancestor of v or an ancestor of g(v). Sweep equal-weight buckets in descending order. An ordered set of Euler positions finds the extreme members of H_v, whose LCA is g(v). For one constraint (v,g), its satisfaction indicator over candidate subtrees is represented by point contributions +1 at v, +1 at g, and -1 at LCA(v,g); a Fenwick subtree sum therefore counts how many active constraints a candidate satisfies. A node is an answer iff it has a higher node outside its subtree and its constraint count equals the number of active constraints. Query and update each equal-weight bucket before inserting it, because inequalities are strict.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n) time and O(n log n) memory per test case for Euler DFS, binary-lifting LCA, ordered-set operations, and Fenwick queries/updates.
tricks: turn every possible larger reply into a necessary-and-sufficient ancestor-union constraint
batch equal weights so the active set contains strictly greater values only
obtain the LCA of a dynamic set from its minimum and maximum Euler positions
encode ancestor(v) OR ancestor(g(v)) by +1 at v, +1 at g(v), and -1 at LCA(v,g(v))
use a separate higher-node count to reject openings that leave the opponent no legal reply

solution_variants.name: descending-weight-lca-two-fenwick
solution_variants.summary: Precompute Euler intervals and binary-lifting LCA. Sweep weights downward in equal-value buckets. One Fenwick tree counts already active higher nodes, an ordered set finds the Euler extrema outside each subtree and hence g(v), and a second signed Fenwick tree maintains how many constraints each candidate subtree satisfies via +v,+g,-LCA(v,g). Query candidates before adding the current bucket.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log n) time and O(n log n) memory.

solution_variants.name: euler-interval-disjunction-sweep
solution_variants.summary: The official implementation avoids explicit LCA constraints. For each processed v it takes the minimum and maximum higher Euler positions outside subtree(v); every lower candidate interval must contain v or both extrema. It maintains the accumulated disjunctions with global interval bounds and a prefix-maximum Fenwick tree while sweeping weights downward.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log n) time and O(n) memory beyond the tree and ordered set.

tags.evidence: The solution first proves exactly when every opponent reply is losing and uses a maximum violating reply to prove necessity.
All nodes are processed by descending weight in equal-value batches, turning every condition over strictly higher values into incremental state.
For each v, g(v) is the LCA of all higher nodes outside subtree(v), and LCA(v,g(v)) is required by the signed constraint update.
Fenwick subtree sums maintain both the count of active higher nodes and the number of ancestor-union constraints satisfied by each candidate.
Euler intervals make ancestor tests and subtree Fenwick sums contiguous and allow H_v to be summarized by outside interval extrema.
An ordered set of active Euler positions supplies the minimum and maximum strictly higher nodes outside a queried subtree in O(log n).
Binary lifting answers the LCA queries used to build g(v) and the inclusion-exclusion update in O(log n).
